Thank you for your letter of January 3, 1990 and the above
referenced infornal assistance. However, it did not provide the
guidance I requested. (ft was also addressed to City Attorney
Biondi (sic) naking ne wonder how that can be since the name is
cLearly printed on the letterhead. )
Your letter concludes that I nay participate in the SDG&E power
plant proposed for Carlsbad unless that would have a reasonably
foreseeable naterial financial effect on Edison. That doesnrt
answer the question. will ny activities in carlsbad, as the city
pursues its efforts to stop construction of the plant, have that
effect and how am I supposed to determine it? 
The status quo is
no power plant. 
The ultimate decision about whether or not one is
to be constructed will be nade by the Energy Commission, the Public
Utilities 
Comnissi.on and the Coastal Comrnission. If all three
state agencies approve the plant it is ny understanding that it can
be constructed notwithstanding Carlsbadrs opposition. 
If 
we
succeed in persuading any of those state agencies to disapprove the
p] ant then +-!:.e s|-atrr-s qrro -i.s maint-ained, Ilow can that have a
material financial effect on SDG&E? If $re are unsuccessful and the
plant is approved by the state agencies it may be constructed in
spite of our efforts so how can our fai.lure have a materiat
financial effect on SDG&E?
I would also appreciate guidance on the foreseeability discussion
which conctudes: rr... that there is a substantial likalihood that
the merger will occur, ...rr f dontt think the power plant decision
in Carlsbad wi1l, have a naterial financial efiect on SOCCE since,
if they donrt build it here they will build it someplace e1se. ft
i.s even less clear to ne how that effect can be attributed to
Southern California Edison. However, since your opinion appears
to turn on the question of whether or not the nerger will 
go
through, I have asked the City Attorney's office of San Diego its
opinion on that matter. ur. c. If. fitzpatrick, 
Assistan€ city




Attorney. rrho is in charge of the city of san Diego's opposition
to the merger, reports in a letter dated January 22, 3.990 that, I'I
obviously do not personally agree that there is a rsubstantial'
likelihood that the proposed merger will occur.rr He reports that
the merger viI1 not be approved by the Public Utilities commission
unless the courpanies carry their burden of proof that the proposal
is in the best interests of the rate payers of both conpanies. He
reports further that the city of san Diego has submitted voluminous
expert testirnony before the Federal Energ-y Regulatory commission
clearly shosing, in the city's view, that the Donetary savings
lrhich the companies clain rrill occur as a rasult of the proposed
nerger are, ". . . speculative, overstated and iLlusory.r! 
Irr.
Fitzpatrick reports furtheE. rr... our experts advise that the
proposal is violative on its face of the federal antitrust lavs and
have so testified before the FERC in docunentary forrn.i 
Mr.
Fitzpatrick concludes that the analysis by Jeevan s. Ahuja of your
legal division that there is a 'rsubstantialrr likelihood is rr...at
least suspect.I' I agree. I think at thj-s point it is impossible
to say whether or not the merger wiII occur. It seehs to me that
before your agency disqualifies the cityrs attorney and thereby
requires the city council to retain expensj-ve outside counsel,
there should be nore to the potential- conflj-ct then nere
speculation.
I also vrould appreciate additional guidance on the materiality
question. I understand vhat the guidelines say. Southern cal
Edison is a nuttibillion dollar conpany and, if the merger is
approved lrill become the nation's largest utility. 
whether or not
one sma1l additional pover plant is located in CarLsbad or
elselrhere in its service territory doesnrt seem to me to involve
a naterial financial effect on a conpany of that size. utility
rate making is an extremely complex natter and I an at a loss to
understand hov I can possibly determine irhether or not if the polrer
plant is located outsj-de carlsbad, that lrill result in a naterial
financiat effect on Edison -
we need additional guidance. Unless you are able to conclude that
the city Attorney nay participate ny city will be forced to retain
outside counsel. Before publicLy naking that recolulendation, I
need to be satiafied that the Fair Political Practices commission
is giving our city council no alternative.
Your prompt attention to this supplemental request for assistance
would be appreciated.
city

Attorney
